lesson.title: Chapter 9 summary and quiz

exercises.title: Safe Integer Division with cin Recovery
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Complete the function `safe_divide` and a `main` that reads pairs of integers, recovers from bad input, and prints either the integer quotient or an error message.

## Input
- Line 1: integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10), the number of division operations to perform.
- Lines 2..N+1: each line contains two values intended to be integers, separated by a space. A line may contain non-integer tokens.

## Output
For each of the N lines print exactly one of the following (no extra blank lines, no trailing spaces):
- If either token on the line is not a valid integer: `Error: invalid input`
- If the second integer is 0: `Error: division by zero`
- Otherwise: `Result: Q` where Q is the integer quotient of the first value divided by the second (truncated toward zero, matching C++ integer division).

## Requirements
1. Implement `int safe_divide(int a, int b)` that uses `assert` to enforce `b != 0` (the caller guarantees this after checking).
2. In `main`, attempt to read two integers with `std::cin`. If extraction fails, call `std::cin.clear()` then `std::cin.ignore(std::numeric_limits<std::streamsize>::max(), '\n')` to recover, then print `Error: invalid input` and continue to the next iteration.
3. If both integers are read successfully but the divisor is 0, print `Error: division by zero` without calling `safe_divide`.
4. Otherwise call `safe_divide` and print the result.
5. The program must handle N = 1 (minimum), all-valid inputs, all-invalid inputs, and a mix.

test_cases.label: Sample — mix of valid, zero divisor, and bad input
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Result: 3
Error: division by zero
Error: invalid input
Result: -3

test_cases.label: Hidden — all error cases
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Error: division by zero
Error: invalid input
Error: division by zero

exercises.title: Constexpr Lookup Table with static_assert and Runtime Bounds Check
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Build a small program that uses `static_assert` to validate a compile-time lookup table of squares and `assert` to guard runtime index access, combining both assertion types from this chapter with `std::cin` recovery from earlier lessons.

## Input
- Line 1: integer Q (1 ≤ Q ≤ 20), the number of lookup operations.
- Lines 2..Q+1: each line contains a single value intended to be an integer index.

## Output
For each operation print exactly one line:
- If the input is not a valid integer: `Error: invalid input`
- If the index is in range [0, 9]: `square(I) = V` where I is the index and V is `I * I`.
- If the index is out of range (< 0 or > 9): `Error: index I out of range` where I is the index.

After all operations, print one final line: `Lookups complete.`

## Requirements
1. Declare a `constexpr int TABLE_SIZE = 10;` constant.
2. Declare a C-style array `constexpr int squares[TABLE_SIZE] = {0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81};` at namespace scope.
3. Add a `static_assert(TABLE_SIZE > 0, "table must have positive size");` at namespace scope.
4. Add a `static_assert(squares[0] == 0, "first square must be 0");` at namespace scope.
5. Add a `static_assert(squares[9] == 81, "last square must be 81");` at namespace scope.
6. Write a function `int lookupSquare(int index)` that uses `assert(index >= 0 && index < TABLE_SIZE && "index out of bounds")` and then returns `squares[index]`.
7. In `main`, read Q, then for each operation:
   - Attempt to read an integer. If extraction fails, clear the error, flush the line, print `Error: invalid input`, and continue.
   - If the index is in range [0, TABLE_SIZE-1], call `lookupSquare` and print the result.
   - If the index is out of range, print the error message (do NOT call `lookupSquare`).
8. After all operations, print `Lookups complete.`
9. Hidden tests cover: Q = 1 with a valid index, all out-of-range indices, a non-integer input, and boundary indices 0 and 9.

test_cases.label: Sample — mix of valid, out-of-range, and bad input
test_cases.expected_stdout:
square(0) = 0
square(5) = 25
Error: index 10 out of range
Error: index -1 out of range
Error: invalid input
Lookups complete.

test_cases.label: Hidden — Q = 1, all out of range
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Error: index 99 out of range
Lookups complete.

fixes_applied.description: Replaced exercise 9.x.2 (Compile-Time Buffer Validator using template<typename T, int N> struct FixedBuffer) with a non-template exercise that uses constexpr arrays, static_assert at namespace scope, assert for runtime bounds checking, and std::cin recovery — combining all major Ch 9 concepts without requiring templates (Ch 11) or structs (Ch 13)

Fixed summary code example: moved static_assert outside constexpr function to namespace/block scope (more idiomatic), replaced std::println/std::format with std::cout, removed <format> and <print> includes
Exercise 9.x.1 (Safe Integer Division with cin Recovery) kept as-is — no forward reference issues found
